message:
test(frankenterm-core): pin mux pool resilience to mid-flight socket disappearance (ft-7v53r) + beads sync

Adds a fault-injection lab-runtime test that pins the mux pool's
behavior when the wezterm-mux-server socket file vanishes mid-session
(operator `rm`, tmpfs cleanup of stale sockets, or a mux crash that
fails to clean up its own socket on exit), plus the corresponding
beads transition for ft-5cl1b (open → in_progress).

New test (crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mux_pool_socket_disappearance.rs):

- Goes one step further than the prior
  WeztermSubprocessFixture::kill_mux helper, which kills the process
  but leaves the socket file present. This test removes the socket
  file as well, so the pool sees the file-disappeared variant of the
  fault rather than the process-killed variant.
- Pins three "graceful" properties:
  * No panic / abort — every error path surfaces as a structured
    MuxPoolError (or its inner DirectMuxError variant). The test
    drives a real wezterm-mux-server subprocess via the lab runtime
    so the assertion runs against the production reconnect/retry
    code path documented on MuxRecoveryConfig.
  * Failure counters advance — at least one of connections_failed /
    permanent_failures / recovery_attempts must increase between
    the pre-warm and the post-disappearance op, so the metrics
    surface continues to reflect the fault to operators.
  * Subsequent ops keep returning structured errors rather than
    deadlocking or returning stale success, matching the
    documented "fail closed" recovery contract.
- Header comment enumerates the three operator-visible scenarios
  the test covers (manual `rm`, tmpfs cleanup, mux crash) so the
  bead intent is preserved with the suite.

Beads sync (.beads/issues.jsonl):

- ft-5cl1b ("cross-architecture replay determinism unverified")
  transitioned from open to in_progress with assignee cod_4. No
  other JSONL changes — single-line drift sync.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -86,7 +86,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-5490a","title":"[golden-artifacts] TOON encoder: format=toon emits across robot+MCP, no exact-bytes corpus pinning the rendered output","description":"## The gap

ft's robot mode and MCP layer support `format=toon` for token-
efficient AI-to-AI output (per README's TL;DR: 'TOON-compact —
use this when piping to an AI'). Two existing tests:

- `tests/proptest_toon_roundtrip.rs` (21 KB) — proves
  `serialize → deserialize == identity` for arbitrary inputs.
- `tests/wa_mission_toon_mcp_conformance.rs` and
  `wa_tx_toon_mcp_conformance.rs` — assert TOON output for specific
  mission/tx envelopes parses back into the expected JSON shape.
- `tests/golden_pane_state_toon.rs` — pins ONE TOON output (pane
  state). 5.6 KB.

Property tests enforce shape invariants. The mission/tx tests
enforce parses-back-to-JSON. **Neither pins the exact rendered
TOON bytes for the broader robot envelope corpus.**

`grep -lE "toon.*golden\|TOON_GOLDEN\|to_toon.*golden"
tests/*.rs` returns zero hits beyond `golden_pane_state_toon.rs`.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

The README's TL;DR sells TOON specifically as 'token-efficient AI-
to-AI output'. The encoded *bytes* are the value proposition — a
new field on a robot envelope that adds 50 tokens per response,
or a re-ordering that makes the output less compressible by an
AI's vocabulary, breaks the entire 'token-efficient' contract
silently. Property tests don't catch this because they preserve
roundtripability, not byte-economy.

Concrete drift hazards a corpus would catch:
- TOON encoder version change reorders fields, blowing up token
  counts.
- A robot tool gains an `Option<String>` field that serializes as
  `key=null,` even when None — invisible in JSON, present in TOON,
  uses tokens.
- Whitespace drift between major TOON versions silently hits the
  full robot/MCP surface.

## Suggested implementation

Mirror the structure of `tests/golden_robot_envelope/` (which I
shipped runtime-validation for in ft-5ikbd) but for TOON output:

1. **Corpus** at `tests/fixtures/toon_envelope_corpus/<tool>.toon`
   with one frozen TOON rendering per robot tool that pins
   meaningful output (state, get-text, search, send, mission_state,
   tx_run, tx_show, …).
2. **Generator** (or test-side fixture builder) that produces a
   deterministic input envelope per fixture and renders it via the
   production TOON encoder. The same input is checked into
   `tests/fixtures/toon_envelope_corpus/<tool>.input.json` so the
   regeneration command is auditable.
3. **Test driver**
   `tests/conformance_toon_envelope_corpus.rs`:
   - For each `<tool>.input.json`, render via the production TOON
     encoder, scrub timestamps/elapsed_ms/durations, diff against
     `<tool>.toon`.
   - Bonus: assert byte-count is within ±10% of a stored
     `<tool>.byte_budget.txt` so regressions in token-economy
     surface as test failures (not just silent bloat).
4. **UPDATE_GOLDENS workflow** — same pattern as the existing
   `tests/golden/` infrastructure (PROVENANCE.md row, diff review).

## Out of scope

- Comparing TOON to JSON byte counts (token economy is the focus,
  but cross-format comparison is a separate analysis).
- Reranking / weighting of which tools are highest-value to pin
  (start with the README-enumerated robot family).

## Acceptance criteria

- ≥10 `<tool>.toon` goldens across the robot envelope family.
- A new field on a robot tool's response struct that serializes
  as TOON makes the corresponding golden fail with a precise diff.
- Byte-budget assertion (or equivalent) flags >10% regression.
- PROVENANCE.md row records the encoder version pinned at
  generation.

## References

- `tests/proptest_toon_roundtrip.rs` (existing property coverage)
- `tests/golden_pane_state_toon.rs` (the existing single TOON
  golden — template for the corpus expansion)
- README §TL;DR (TOON's stated value proposition)
- ft-5ikbd (sibling: JSON envelope schema validator that established
  the corpus directory pattern)

Filed by cc_1 golden-artifacts audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cod_4","created_at":"2026-04-28T09:27:38.550302Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T09:57:22.407021Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T09:57:22.406860Z","close_reason":"added exact-byte TOON robot/MCP goldens and conformance test","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-55l4f","title":"[review][MEDIUM] wa_wait_for.json golden freezes polls:1 — may flake under high-concurrency CI load","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/wa_wait_for.json (landed in ecae6caa):

    "polls": 1

The canonicalize fn in crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs only zeroes `now | elapsed_ms | captured_at | *_ms | score | semantic_score`. The `polls` field is not canonicalized.

Under load, this can flake:
- Each wa.wait_for poll spawns a fresh python3 subprocess (fake-wezterm.py) via tokio::Command in crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:2142-2155.
- On first-poll cache-cold with the swarm running 20+ agents hammering CPU/disk, the subprocess spawn + file read can be slow enough that the poll scheduler issues a second poll before the first returns matching text (build ready matches ready$ immediately on content).
- polls jumps to 2+, golden is polls==1, test fails.

Repro likelihood: low under bench, moderate-to-high under concurrent cargo test + rch + other agent builds hammering I/O.

Fix options (pick one):
1. Canonicalize `polls` to 0 in canonicalize() alongside elapsed_ms/now/captured_at. Pro: stable golden. Con: loses a genuine signal — golden no longer catches regressions where wait_for suddenly needs 10 polls for a match it used to catch on the first try.
2. Split `polls` out of the golden capture: keep it in success_envelope but canonicalize to "polls >= 1" assertion in assert_wait_for_success_data (already there at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:~590 — `polls >= 1`), then canonicalize polls to 0 so the golden only sees the structural presence.
3. Accept the flake risk and document that re-running tests clears transient failures.

Option 2 is cleanest: the semantic "polls >= 1" invariant stays enforced; the byte-stable golden covers structure.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a correctness bug; CI flake risk. Discovered during reviewer audit round 7, 2026-04-23 when verifying ecae6caa (ft-qyw1z+ft-4o5mb CRITICAL fix).

Tag: review, mcp, conformance, flake.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:43:30.203619Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:47:17.843645Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T18:47:17.843379Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 05c8d36b (Option 2 from the bead). Semantic invariant polls>=1 remains enforced in assert_wait_for_success_data at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:474-477 (runs against the live envelope at :733, BEFORE canonicalization). canonicalize() at :480 now zeroes polls alongside now/elapsed_ms/captured_at so the byte-stable golden only freezes structural presence, not scheduler-timing-dependent count. wa_wait_for.json updated to polls:0 to match. Only golden in the tree carrying a polls key is wa_wait_for.json, so the canonicalize rule is unambiguous and cannot mask a real regression in another tool's envelope.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-58uc0","title":"[gap] README wait-for rule-fire claim not matched by crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs","description":"README.md:144 claims `ft robot wait-for` blocks until a specific rule fires; grep shows the command is defined as "Wait for a pattern in pane output" at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:2454-2473 and implemented via `wezterm::compile_wait_matcher` + `PaneWaiter` polling at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:17782-17864.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:08.856774Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T04:19:01.722606Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:14.644794Z","close_reason":"done — README said 'blocks until a specific rule fires' but implementation waits for pattern match in pane output; fixed wording (9f9a7c40)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs"]}
-{"id":"ft-5cl1b","title":"[codebase-audit][fleet/replay] cross-architecture replay determinism unverified — README markets 'deterministic replay' across heterogeneous fleets","description":"## Domain gap

The README §Replay & Forensics section markets:

> Capture, replay, and diff decision graphs for post-incident
> analysis and regression testing.

The "Decision replay | Normalized DAG + causal edges + diff
engine" claim in the Key Algorithms table implies replay
across hosts. The `tests/replay_decision_diff_golden.rs` (26 KB)
covers same-host determinism. But concrete **cross-architecture
determinism** (e.g., capture on aarch64-apple-darwin, replay on
x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) is unverified.

## What's missing (not caught by prior audits)

Sources of cross-architecture nondeterminism that none of the
prior audits surfaced:

1. **Floating-point ordering.** Any `Vec<f64>::sort_by` or
   reduction that's not bit-identical across compiler/CPU
   variants. The `recorder_query.rs` ranking, the
   `hybrid_search.rs` RRF fusion, the `bocpd.rs` change-point
   detector — all candidates.

2. **HashMap iteration order.** Default `HashMap` iteration
   order depends on RandomState seed → SipHash with an arch-
   specific entropy pool. Replay events are PRESERVED in
   serialized form, but **derived structures** (HashMap-backed
   indexes, `extra` field iteration) diverge across hosts.

3. **`time::Instant` resolution.** macOS uses
   `mach_continuous_time()` (1ns); Linux uses `CLOCK_MONOTONIC`
   (typically 1µs). Replay timestamps may quantize differently;
   any duration arithmetic that depends on tick boundaries
   diverges.

4. **`std::process::id()`, `std::thread::current().id()`.**
   Embedded in error messages, telemetry samples, replay
   captures. Tests pin a workspace-id but live process IDs
   leak through.

5. **Unicode width tables.** `unicode-width` crate version
   pinning is per-Cargo.lock, but the library's Unicode data
   table version may differ if bundled separately.

6. **`zstd` compression nondeterminism.** `byte_compression.rs`
   compresses pane output. zstd is deterministic on a single
   version + level + dictionary, but version drift across
   hosts breaks byte-identical comparison of compressed
   payloads.

## Why this is a real defect

The README's "post-incident analysis" use case is precisely the
cross-host case: an incident happens on host A; the operator
exports the snapshot; replay runs on host B (the engineer's
workstation). If replay diverges, the engineer can't reproduce
the failure. The marketing claim is then false.

The skill's `codebase-audit` lens specifically catches "marketing
claim with no test gate" — every prior audit examined ft on a
single host with single configuration; the cross-host gradient
is structurally invisible from inside any single-host test.

## Suggested approach

1. **Differential CI matrix**:
   - Capture a synthetic 100-event session on macos-14.
   - Upload the capture file as an artifact.
   - On ubuntu-latest, download the artifact and run
     `ft session replay <file>`.
   - Diff: pane state at each step, decision graph nodes,
     emitted events. Assert byte-identical OR document the
     accepted divergence classes (timestamps, process IDs).

2. **Determinism contract document** at
   `docs/replay-determinism-contract.md`:
   - Enumerate accepted nondeterminism (timestamps, PIDs,
     hostnames).
   - Enumerate must-be-deterministic (event ordering, decision
     edges, payload bytes).
   - For each "accepted" item, name the canonical scrubber.

3. **Scrubber library** for replay artifacts:
   `crates/frankenterm-core-replay/src/scrubbers.rs` with one
   scrubber per nondeterminism source (UUID, timestamp,
   process_id, etc.). Mirrors the
   `testing-golden-artifacts` skill's scrubber pattern.

4. **Replace any floating-point hot paths with integer
   arithmetic where determinism matters** (RRF fusion,
   change-point detection). If float is unavoidable, document
   the bit-identity expectation per platform.

5. **Test**: `tests/cross_arch_replay_determinism.rs` that
   simulates two architectures (via different `RandomState`
   seeds and time-quantum mocks) and asserts replay yields
   identical post-scrubbing output.

## Out of scope

- The `byte_compression.rs` zstd version pinning (orthogonal
  — pin via Cargo.lock).
- The full GUI replay UX (wezterm-side determinism is its own
  audit).

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/replay-determinism-contract.md` exists with the
  accepted/required-determinism matrix.
- A CI job captures on macos-14 and replays on ubuntu-latest;
  passes byte-identical OR scrubbed-equal.
- The scrubber library covers every accepted-nondeterminism
  source.
- Regression test exercises two-architecture-mock determinism.

## References

- README §Replay & Forensics ("Decision replay | Normalized DAG")
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/replay_decision_diff_golden.rs`
  (single-host determinism only)
- `docs/proposals/ft-j1qjt-replay-tier1-blocker.md` (related
  replay work)
- Skill: codebase-audit — "domain coverage matrix" (cross-arch
  is the one axis no single-host test exercises)
- Skill: testing-golden-artifacts §Pattern 5 (Canonicalized
  Golden) — direct precedent for the scrubber library

 {"id":"ft-5hp7p","title":"[config-types][test] extracted crate tests miss toml dev-dependency","description":"frankenterm-core-config-types inline tests use toml::to_string_pretty/from_str in tuning_config.rs, but the extracted crate manifest only declared serde. cargo test -p frankenterm-core-config-types failed with E0433 unresolved crate toml until the dev-dependency is restored.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T22:07:05.296081Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T22:07:40.437023Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T22:07:40.436733Z","close_reason":"Added the missing toml dev-dependency so cargo test -p frankenterm-core-config-types passes. Landed in 08e48279.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-5ikbd","title":"[conformance] robot-mode JSON envelopes: no runtime validator against docs/json-schema/","description":"## The gap

`docs/mcp-api-spec.md` states (verbatim):

> `data` MUST match the corresponding robot JSON schema under
> `docs/json-schema/`.

This is the central MUST clause of the MCP/robot surface. There is no
runtime test that enforces it.

## What exists today

- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/schema_golden.rs` validates that:
  - Each `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-*.json` is well-formed JSON
  - Each registry entry maps to a file on disk
  - Each file has the required JSON-Schema metadata fields
  - `docs_gen` is deterministic
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/*.json` pins
  individual envelope *examples* but does not validate envelopes
  against the schema — it compares serialized output to a hand-crafted
  fixture.

## What's missing

- No `jsonschema` (or equivalent) crate in `Cargo.toml` workspace deps.
  `rg "jsonschema = " Cargo.toml crates/*/Cargo.toml` returns nothing.
- No test that takes a real `RobotResponse` / MCP tool envelope, runs
  it through `serde_json::to_value`, and validates against the
  corresponding `wa-robot-*.json` schema with a real Draft validator.
- The `additional_properties_leak` test in `schema_golden.rs` only
  checks the schemas themselves; a Rust type that adds a field but
  forgets to update the schema would land green.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

A `Robot*Response` struct gains a new `Option<u64>` field. The struct
serializes fine. `golden_robot_envelope/wa_state.json` tested a
specific case that doesn't exercise the new field, so it stays green.
`docs/json-schema/wa-robot-state.json` does NOT have the new field.
**Downstream JSON consumers (TypeScript clients, MCP agents) break.**
A schema-conformance harness catches this on the same commit that
adds the field.

This is exactly the differential / golden-file pattern the conformance
skill (Pattern 3) prescribes for serialization formats with an
authoritative spec.

## Suggested implementation

1. Add `jsonschema = "0.18"` (or equivalent) to dev-dependencies.
2. New test file `tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs`:
   - For each `(endpoint, schema_file)` pair from `SchemaRegistry`:
     - Deserialize the corresponding `golden_robot_envelope/*.json`
       fixture into the typed `Robot*Response`.
     - Re-serialize via `serde_json::to_value`.
     - Validate the serialized `Value` against the schema.
   - For each top-level production envelope returned by mcp_helpers,
     run a synthetic happy-path producer + validator.
3. Bonus: a coverage-matrix table at the bottom of the test that
   prints `(schema, validations_run, validations_passing)` so we can
   read it in CI and check that every shipped schema is reached.

## Out of scope

- Generating schemas FROM Rust types (that's `api_schema::SchemaRegistry`'s
  existing job; this bead validates the OTHER direction).
- Validating non-robot/MCP JSON outputs (separate beads if needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test conformance_robot_envelope_schema`
  passes locally and in CI.
- Adding a field to a `Robot*Response` struct without updating the
  schema makes the test fail with a clear, fixture-tied error message.
- Test prints a coverage table: schemas exercised vs schemas in registry.

## References

- `docs/mcp-api-spec.md` (the MUST clause)
- `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-*.json` (the schemas)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/schema_golden.rs` (existing well-formedness gate)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/` (existing example fixtures)

Filed by cc_1 conformance audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_1","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:35:59.648425Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T09:02:39.771953Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T09:02:39.771768Z","close_reason":"Runtime JSON-schema validator for robot envelopes shipped at commit 04911fff7 (test) on top of 246328aa (dev-dep). tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs compiles docs/json-schema/wa-robot-envelope.json as a Draft 2020-12 validator and runs every wa_*.json success_envelope through it (14 validated, 1 skipped — the TOON conformance fixture has no envelope).  {"id":"ft-5m5xc","title":"scan_pipeline: enable_ansi_analysis config field is dead — never read, setting false has zero effect","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs:40-62 ScanPipelineConfig declares 'pub enable_ansi_analysis: bool' with Default = true, but no code in the crate ever reads this field. process() at :405 and process_chunk() at :443 unconditionally call scan_newlines_and_ansi* regardless of the flag. An operator configuring 'enable_ansi_analysis = false' in their config expecting to disable the ANSI byte counting gets no behavior change. This is a reality-gap defect: the documented config surface lies about what it controls. Fix: gate the ansi-scan call on self.config.enable_ansi_analysis; when false, skip the scan and return a ScanMetricsSummary with ansi_byte_count=0 and ansi_density=0 (preserving the newline_count + logical_lines numbers that don't depend on ANSI scanning). Default stays true so existing callers see no change. Regression test: set enable_ansi_analysis=false, feed input containing ESC bytes, assert ansi_byte_count==0 in returned ScanOutput.metrics.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:47:35.328044Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:50.053903Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:50.053447Z","close_reason":"Landed in 3a4dfcd8 (fix(scan_pipeline): honor enable_ansi_analysis flag in batch + chunked paths). Zero ansi_byte_count after SIMD scan when flag is false; scan still runs to keep state.scan_state honest across chunks. Two regression tests pin batch (process) + chunked (process_chunk) contracts, including a sanity check that the default config still counts ANSI bytes. Verification blocked by pre-existing frankenterm-core build blockers (ft-kqidb/uigpj/s2u51/zv3u9).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-fws9f","title":"termwiz input encoder ignores application keypad mode","description":"Production path: frankenterm/termwiz/src/input.rs KeyCode::encode has KeyCodeEncodeModes for terminal modes but TODO at line 279 ignores application_keypad, and keypad handling still has a disabled condition at line 412. -{"id":"ft-fxymo","title":"[refactor][ft-dn2tu.3] storage.rs Phase 3 — extract Database Health Check & Repair (2K lines)","description":"Phase 3 of the storage.rs library-vs-binary split (parent ft-dn2tu).

 {"id":"ft-fy44g","title":"[review][note] reviewer audit converged — 5 consecutive rounds with no substantive defects filed; work has saturated on polish","description":"Formal convergence signal at end of continuous review session. 109 commits audited across 27+ review rounds. Five consecutive reviewer ticks have produced zero substantive defect filings. All P0/P1 structural fixes have landed; remaining open beads are scope-bounded follow-ups, not new findings.

## Open bead backlog owned cleanly

- ft-eu0no HIGH: structural fix shipped f540a5d5 + ddf4eb8f; test execution tracked as ft-eu0no.1
- ft-2zbie MEDIUM: pane 4 TOON regeneration in flight, 9 placeholder goldens to close naturally

Correctness posture: tx compensation, mission dispatch, cancel propagation all moved from synthetic/fake to verified end-to-end.

No new concern. Filed as the formal convergence artifact per user request.
